name: Chicken Provencal
steps: Cook quinoa per package directions.
Sear chicken in olive oil 4-5 min per side, remove.
Saute shallot and tomatoes 3-4 min with oregano, return chicken to warm through.
Serve over quinoa.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken with salt, pepper, and half the herbes de Provence.
Heat olive oil in a skillet over medium-high heat. Sear the chicken 4-5 minutes per side until golden, then remove to a plate.
Add the shallot to the same pan and cook 2 minutes until softened, then add garlic and cook 30 seconds.
Pour in the wine, scraping up the browned bits, and simmer 1-2 minutes.
Add the tomatoes, olives, and remaining herbes de Provence. Return the chicken to the pan, spooning some sauce over it.
Cover and simmer 12-15 minutes until the chicken is cooked through (165F) and the tomatoes have broken down into a light sauce.
Cook the quinoa per package directions and serve the chicken and sauce over it.
description: A rustic chicken dish built on the classic Provençal flavors of tomato, olive, and herbes de Provence.

name: Salmon with Dijon Cream Sauce
steps: Cook rice per package directions.
Bake salmon at 400F for 12-15 min.
Whisk Dijon and yogurt for the sauce, roast or saute zucchini.
Plate salmon with sauce, zucchini, and rice.
steps_detailed: Season the salmon with salt and pepper. Heat a lightly oiled skillet over medium-high heat and sear the salmon 3-4 minutes per side until it flakes easily. Remove to a plate.
In the same pan, add the shallot and cook 1-2 minutes until softened.
Pour in the wine and simmer 1 minute, then whisk in the mustard, yogurt, and cream. Warm gently over low heat — don't let it boil, or the yogurt can split.
Stir in the dill.
Meanwhile, cook the rice per package directions and saute the zucchini in a separate pan with olive oil, 5-6 minutes until tender.
Spoon the sauce over the salmon and serve with rice and zucchini.
description: A simple salmon dish built on real dijon mustard, a classic French pairing.

name: French Onion Chicken
steps: Cook quinoa per package directions.
Caramelize onion slowly in a pan, 12-15 min, deglaze with balsamic.
Sear chicken, top with onions and cheese, melt under broiler 2 min.
Serve over quinoa.
steps_detailed: Melt the butter in a skillet over medium-low heat. Add the onion and cook slowly, stirring occasionally, for 20-25 minutes until deeply caramelized and jammy — don't rush this, it's what makes the dish taste like French onion soup.
Season the chicken with salt, pepper, and thyme. Push the onions to the side of the pan, raise heat to medium-high, and sear the chicken 4-5 minutes per side.
Add the broth and balsamic vinegar, scraping up any browned bits. Spoon the caramelized onions back over the chicken.
Top with gruyere, cover, and let it melt over low heat for 2-3 minutes.
Cook the quinoa per package directions and serve alongside.
description: A weeknight dish borrowing French onion soup's deeply caramelized onions and gruyère.

name: Herbed White Bean & Chicken Skillet
steps: Sear chicken with garlic and oregano until cooked through, 6-7 min.
Add beans, warm through 3-4 min, drizzle with olive oil.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken with salt and pepper. Heat olive oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and brown the chicken 5-6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until cooked through. Remove to a plate.
Add the shallot to the pan and cook 2 minutes, then add garlic and cook 30 seconds.
Add the beans, broth, oregano, and thyme. Simmer 5-6 minutes, mashing a few of the beans against the pan to naturally thicken the sauce.
Return the chicken to the pan and warm through. Finish with fresh parsley.
description: A simple, herb-forward chicken and white bean skillet.

name: French Chicken & White Bean Jar
steps: Layer dressing, beans, tomato, and chicken in a jar.
Shake before eating.
steps_detailed: Whisk the balsamic vinegar and olive oil together.
Toss the beans, tomatoes, and shallot with half the dressing.
Layer in a jar, top with the chicken and parsley, and drizzle with remaining dressing before sealing.
description: A simple, portable chicken and white bean salad.

name: Coq au Vin
steps: Sear chicken until browned.
Add onion, carrot, and wine, simmer covered 25-30 min until tender.
steps_detailed: Cook the bacon in a heavy pot over medium heat until crisp, 3-4 minutes. Remove with a slotted spoon, leaving the fat in the pot.
Season the chicken and sear in the bacon fat, 3-4 minutes per side until browned. Remove.
Add the onion, mushrooms, and carrot to the pot, cooking 5-6 minutes, then add garlic and tomato paste, cooking 1 minute more.
Pour in the red wine, scraping up the browned bits, and add thyme and bay leaf.
Return the chicken and bacon to the pot. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook on low 30-35 minutes until the chicken is fork-tender and the sauce has taken on a deep reddish-brown color.
Remove the bay leaf before serving. If the sauce is thin, uncover for the last 10 minutes to reduce.
description: Coq au vin means 'rooster in wine' - specifically red Burgundy wine, which gives the dish its deep reddish-brown color and body. White wine changes the whole character of the sauce; mushrooms and pearl onions are also classic additions that were missing.

name: French Onion Baked Chicken
steps: Caramelize onion 12-15 min.
Top chicken with onion and cheese, bake at 375F for 20-22 min with broth in the pan.
steps_detailed: Melt butter in an oven-safe skillet over medium-low heat. Add the onion and cook slowly, stirring occasionally, 20-25 minutes until deeply caramelized.
Preheat the oven to 375F. Season the chicken with salt, pepper, and thyme.
Push the onions aside, raise heat, and sear the chicken 3-4 minutes per side.
Pour the broth around the chicken, top with the caramelized onions and gruyere.
Transfer to the oven and bake 15-18 minutes until the chicken reaches 165F and the cheese is bubbling and golden.
description: Chicken baked with the same deeply caramelized onion and gruyère that defines French onion soup.

name: Salmon en Papillote
steps: Wrap salmon and zucchini with lemon juice and dill in parchment.
Bake at 400F for 15-18 min.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 400F. Cut a large piece of parchment paper and fold it in half to mark a center crease.
Arrange the zucchini, carrot, and shallot on one half of the parchment, drizzle with olive oil, then place the salmon on top.
Drizzle with lemon juice and wine, and scatter the dill over everything.
Fold the parchment over and crimp the edges tightly to seal, creating a sealed packet — this traps steam so the fish cooks gently in its own moisture.
Place on a baking sheet and bake 15-18 minutes. The packet will puff up when done.
Carefully open the packet at the table (watch for escaping steam) and serve directly from the parchment.
description: En papillote, cooking sealed in parchment so the fish steams in its own juices, a classic French technique.

name: Beef Bourguignon
steps: Sear beef until browned.
Add carrot, potato, and wine, simmer covered 30-35 min until tender.
steps_detailed: Cook the bacon in a heavy pot over medium heat until crisp. Remove with a slotted spoon, leaving the fat.
Pat the beef dry, season generously, and sear in batches in the bacon fat until deeply browned on all sides, 4-5 minutes total.
Add the onion, carrot, and mushrooms, cooking 5-6 minutes, then garlic and tomato paste, cooking 1 more minute.
Pour in the red wine and broth, scraping up the browned bits, and add thyme and bay leaf.
Return the beef and bacon to the pot along with the potatoes. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and braise on low (or in a 300F oven) for 1.5-2 hours, until the beef is fork-tender and the sauce is a deep reddish-brown.
Remove the bay leaf. If the sauce needs thickening, simmer uncovered for the last 15 minutes.
description: Bourguignon means 'from Burgundy' - the dish is defined by braising in that region's red wine, not white. Mushrooms and pearl onions are also classic additions this version was missing.

name: Ratatouille
steps: Saute eggplant, zucchini, and pepper in oil 8-10 min.
Add tomatoes, simmer 15 min until soft.
steps_detailed: Salt the cubed eggplant and let it sit in a colander for 15 minutes, then pat dry — this draws out excess moisture and bitterness.
Heat 1 tablespoon oil in a large pot over medium heat. Cook the onion 4-5 minutes until softened, then add garlic and cook 30 seconds.
Add the remaining oil, then the eggplant, zucchini, and bell pepper. Cook 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until starting to soften.
Stir in the tomato paste, then add the diced tomatoes and herbes de Provence.
Simmer uncovered 20-25 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the vegetables are very tender and the sauce has thickened.
Stir in fresh basil at the end. Season with salt and pepper to taste.
description: Ratatouille, a stewed vegetable dish from Provence, is built on herbes de Provence and a long, patient simmer that melds the vegetables together.

name: Nicoise Salad Jar
steps: Layer tuna, green beans, egg, and olives in a jar.
steps_detailed: Blanch the green beans in boiling water for 2-3 minutes until bright green and just tender, then plunge into ice water to stop cooking.
Whisk the olive oil and vinegar together.
Layer the potato, green beans, and tomatoes in the bottom of a jar, drizzling with a little dressing.
Top with tuna, egg quarters, and olives. Add remaining dressing before sealing.
description: A portable version of salade niçoise, built on the classic combination of tuna, olives, and egg.

name: French Ham and Cheese Wrap Jar
steps: Spread mustard on tortilla, layer turkey and cheese, roll and slice.
steps_detailed: Spread mustard evenly over the tortilla.
Layer the turkey, swiss cheese, and lettuce.
Roll tightly and slice in half.
description: The flavors of jambon-beurre in portable form.

name: French Lentil Salad
steps: Toss lentils and carrot with vinegar and oil.
steps_detailed: Whisk the vinegar, mustard, and olive oil together for the vinaigrette.
Toss the warm cooked lentils with the shallot and dressing — dressing lentils while still warm helps them soak up more flavor than dressing them cold.
Stir in the carrot and parsley. Serve warm or chilled.
description: French green lentils (lentilles du Puy), which hold their shape well, are the classic base for this salad.

name: French Herb Omelette
steps: Whisk eggs, cook in a pan, fold in cheese and parsley as it sets.
steps_detailed: Whisk the eggs with a pinch of salt until no streaks remain.
Melt butter in a nonstick skillet over medium-low heat, swirling to coat.
Pour in the eggs. As they set at the edges, use a spatula to gently push the cooked egg toward the center, tilting the pan to let uncooked egg flow to the edges.
When mostly set but still slightly glossy on top, sprinkle the cheese and herbs over half the omelette.
Fold the other half over, let it sit 30 seconds to finish setting and melt the cheese, then slide onto a plate.
description: A simple herb omelette, a French breakfast staple.

name: Endive with Goat Cheese Snack
steps: Top leaves with goat cheese.
steps_detailed: Spread a small amount of goat cheese onto each leaf.
Top with a few chopped walnuts and a light drizzle of honey.
description: Endive and goat cheese, a classic French pairing.

name: French Radish Butter Snack
steps: Serve radishes with a small dollop of butter.
steps_detailed: Serve the radish halves with a small dish of softened butter for dipping — this is a classic French bistro snack, the pepperiness of the radish balanced by the butter's richness.
A light sprinkle of flaky salt on the buttered side takes it further, if you have it.
description: Radishes with butter and salt, a simple, classic French snack.

name: Creme Caramel
steps: Melt 2 tbsp sugar until amber, pour into ramekins.
Whisk eggs, milk, remaining sugar, and vanilla; pour over caramel.
Bake in a water bath at 325F for 35-40 min, chill before serving.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 325F.
Melt about two-thirds of the sugar in a small saucepan over medium heat, swirling (don't stir) until it turns amber. Immediately pour into ramekins, tilting to coat the bottom.
Warm the milk until steaming but not boiling.
Whisk the eggs, remaining sugar, and vanilla together, then slowly whisk in the warm milk.
Strain through a fine mesh sieve for the smoothest texture, then pour over the caramel in the ramekins.
Place the ramekins in a baking dish, add hot water halfway up their sides, and bake 35-40 minutes until just set with a slight jiggle in the center.
Chill at least 3 hours. To unmold, run a knife around the edge and invert onto a plate.
description: Crème caramel, an egg custard with a layer of caramelized sugar, found across French and wider European dessert traditions.

name: Chocolate Mousse
steps: Whisk all ingredients until smooth.
Chill 30 min before serving.
steps_detailed: Melt the chocolate chips gently, either over a double boiler or in the microwave in 15-second bursts, stirring between each, until smooth. Let cool slightly.
Whisk the egg yolks with half the sugar and the vanilla until pale and thick, then whisk in the melted chocolate.
Whip the cream to soft peaks and fold into the chocolate-yolk mixture.

In a separate, completely clean bowl, whip the egg whites with the remaining sugar until they hold stiff, glossy peaks - any trace of grease or yolk in this bowl will stop the whites from whipping properly.
Fold the whipped whites into the chocolate mixture in two or three additions, folding gently to keep as much air in as possible rather than stirring it out.
Spoon into cups and chill at least 2 hours until set - real mousse should feel light and airy, not dense, when you eat it.
Note: this uses raw egg whites and yolks, which carry a small salmonella risk - pasteurized eggs are a safer choice if serving to anyone pregnant, elderly, very young, or immunocompromised.
description: Mousse means 'foam' - real chocolate mousse gets its airy structure from whipped egg whites folded through melted chocolate, not from yogurt standing in for that whole technique. This version builds the real thing.

name: Poached Pears
steps: Simmer pear in water with honey and cinnamon 12-15 min until tender.
steps_detailed: Combine 1 cup water, honey, cinnamon, vanilla, and lemon juice in a small saucepan and bring to a simmer.
Add the pear halves cut-side down and simmer 12-15 minutes, turning once, until a knife slides in easily.
Remove the pears and simmer the poaching liquid another 3-4 minutes to reduce slightly into a light syrup.
Serve the pears warm or chilled, drizzled with the syrup.
description: Pears poached in a simple syrup, a classic French dessert technique.

name: Classic Vinaigrette
steps: Whisk mustard and vinegar, slowly whisk in oil until emulsified.
steps_detailed: Whisk the mustard, vinegar, and shallot together in a small bowl.
While whisking constantly, slowly drizzle in the olive oil — adding it gradually is what lets the vinaigrette emulsify into a thick, cohesive dressing instead of separating.
Season with salt and pepper. This keeps in the fridge for about a week — just re-whisk before using, since it will separate on standing.
description: A simple, classic French vinaigrette.

name: Croque Monsieur
steps: Spread mustard on bread, layer ham and half the cheese, top with second slice.
Top with remaining cheese, bake at 400F for 8-10 min until melted and golden.
steps_detailed: Make a quick bechamel: melt butter in a small saucepan over medium heat, whisk in flour, and cook 1 minute. Slowly whisk in the milk and cook, whisking, until thickened, about 3-4 minutes. Season with a pinch of salt and pepper.
Preheat the oven to 400F.
Spread mustard on one slice of bread, layer with ham and half the gruyere, and top with the second slice.
Spread a spoonful of bechamel over the top of the sandwich and sprinkle with the remaining gruyere.
Bake on a sheet for 8-10 minutes until the cheese is melted and bubbling and the top is golden.
description: Croque monsieur, a ham and gruyère sandwich topped with béchamel, is a French bistro staple - the béchamel on top, not just melted cheese, is what actually separates it from an ordinary grilled ham and cheese.

name: Jambon-Beurre
steps: Spread butter generously on the split baguette.
Layer in the ham.
steps_detailed: Split the baguette piece lengthwise, being careful not to cut all the way through.
Spread the softened butter generously and evenly across both cut sides — this is the whole point of the sandwich, so don't skimp.
Layer in the ham, folding the slices slightly rather than laying them flat, for better texture.
That's it — no mustard, no lettuce. The simplicity is the point.
description: Jambon-beurre, ham and butter on a baguette, is considered France's most iconic, simplest sandwich - the quality of the butter and bread matters more than any other ingredient.

name: French Fruit Salad (Salade de Fruits)
steps: Toss all fruit together with honey.
steps_detailed: Toss the diced apple and pear with lemon juice right away to prevent browning.
Combine with the grapes and orange segments.
Drizzle with honey and toss gently to coat.
description: A simple fresh fruit salad.

name: French Onion Soup
steps: Caramelize onion slowly, 20-25 min, stirring often.
Add broth, simmer 10 min.
Top with toasted bread and cheese, broil until melted.
steps_detailed: Melt the butter in a heavy pot over medium-low heat. Add the onions and cook low and slow, stirring every few minutes, for 30-35 minutes until deeply caramelized to a rich mahogany brown — this is the whole soul of the soup, so resist rushing it with higher heat.
Add the sherry and scrape up any browned bits stuck to the pot.
Add the broth, thyme, and bay leaf. Simmer 15-20 minutes to let the flavors meld.
Remove the bay leaf and ladle into an oven-safe bowl.
Top with the toasted bread and gruyere, then broil for 2-3 minutes until the cheese is melted, bubbling, and browned in spots.
description: French onion soup depends on deeply, patiently caramelized onions - real dry sherry adding a depth that a simpler broth alone wouldn't have.
